title: Face Detection vs. Recognition
concept: Understanding the differences, evaluation metrics, and interaction between face detection and recognition models is key to building face analysis systems.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main difference between face verification and identification?

options.text: Verification is 1:1 matching; identification is 1:N searching.
Verification is done on CPUs; identification is done on GPUs.
Verification detects the face; identification crops it.

exercises.explain: Correct! Verification compares one face to another; identification searches for a face in a database.

cards.title: Working together
cards.body: When you tag friends in a social media photo, the app first highlights their faces with boxes, and then suggests names. This demonstrates how detection and recognition operate as a team: detection finds the candidate coordinates, and recognition analyzes the contents.

cards.title: The Pipeline Stages
cards.body: In a face analysis system, Face Detection acts as the frontend filter (finding crop boxes). Face Recognition acts as the backend processor, analyzing the cropped face pixels. If detection fails, recognition cannot run!

cards.title: Envelopes and Letters
cards.body: Face detection is like finding envelopes in a mailbox: you look for rectangular shapes. Face recognition is like opening each envelope and reading the signature on the letter to find out who sent it.

cards.title: Different error metrics
cards.body: A detector is evaluated on Intersection over Union (IoU) (how well the box overlaps the face). A recognition model is evaluated on False Accept Rate (FAR) (mistaking a stranger for a user) and False Reject Rate (FRR).

cards.title: One model does not do both
cards.body: A common myth is that there is a single neural network called 'Face AI' that takes a raw image and outputs a name. In practice, using separate, specialized models for detection and recognition is much more accurate and modular.

left.text:
IoU (Overlap accuracy)
Precision / Recall of boxes
Tolerates slight shifts

right.text:
FAR (False Accept Rate)
FRR (False Reject Rate)
Requires high precision

exercises.prompt: If a face recognition model mistakenly allows an unauthorized person to unlock a secure phone, what metric has failed?
exercises.explain: A false accept occurs when the system incorrectly matches an unauthorized face to an authorized profile.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If your face detection model fails to locate a face in a photo, the face recognition model can still identify the person.
exercises.explain: False. Recognition depends on detection to crop and provide the face pixels; if detection fails, recognition has nothing to analyze.

exercises.sentence: While detection accuracy is measured using IoU, recognition accuracy is measured using ___ rates.
exercises.explain: Recognition accuracy is evaluated using error metrics like False Accept and False Reject rates.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a metric specifically used to evaluate a face detection model's box accuracy?

options.text: Intersection over Union (IoU) of the predicted bounding boxes
False Accept Rate (FAR) of the identity matching

exercises.explain: IoU measures the spatial overlap between the predicted face box and the ground truth box.

exercises.prompt: You are optimizing a security camera system. You notice that the system is failing to recognize people because the face crop boundaries are cut off at the ears. Which part of the system needs adjustment?

options.text: The face detection model or its bounding box padding, to ensure the entire face is cropped cleanly.
The face recognition database, to add more photos.
The distance metric used to compare facial embeddings.

exercises.explain: Exactly! If the crops are incomplete, the detector is the bottleneck. Adjusting detection parameters or padding the boxes before sending them to recognition is the correct fix. Next: how to align those cropped faces.
